clinical_coherence.notes: 48M with undiagnosed HIV/AIDS (CD4 45) presenting with subacute (4-week) headache, oral thrush, wasting, absent meningismus, and deceptively clear CSF. The absent meningismus, low-grade fever (37.4), and minimal CSF inflammation despite severe infection perfectly reflect the impaired immune response of advanced AIDS. Gelatinous pseudocysts on MRI, bilateral CN VI palsy with papilledema from elevated ICP, and visual obscurations form a flawless clinical picture of cryptococcal meningoencephalitis.

tool_output_consistency.notes: CSF shows markedly elevated OP (38 cmH2O), minimal pleocytosis (WBC 35, 90% lymphocytes), mildly elevated protein (78), borderline low glucose (32). India ink positive, CSF CrAg 1:2048. Serum CrAg 1:1024. MRI shows gelatinous pseudocysts and hydrocephalus without meningeal enhancement. All outputs are perfectly concordant for cryptococcal meningitis in AIDS.

ground_truth_quality.notes: ICD B45.1 correct for cryptococcal meningitis. Critical actions correctly emphasize LP with opening pressure measurement, serial therapeutic LPs for ICP management, antifungal induction therapy, and crucially delaying ART 4-6 weeks to prevent IRIS. Contraindicated actions (immediate ART, corticosteroids) are clinically vital.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Superb diagnostic puzzle: absent meningismus, no fever, clear CSF, minimal inflammation -- all can mislead away from meningitis. Undiagnosed HIV/AIDS must be recognized from the oral thrush, wasting, leukopenia, and social history clues.

demographic_plausibility.notes: 48M African American, MSM who hasn't seen a physician in 8 years, recently separated from male partner. This is a tragically realistic demographic for late-presenting HIV/AIDS with opportunistic infections in the US.

red_herring_quality.notes: Three excellent red herrings: absent neck stiffness (may exclude meningitis), low-grade fever (may suggest non-infectious cause), and deceptively clear CSF (may falsely reassure). Each is a well-documented clinical pitfall in cryptococcal meningitis.

educational_value.notes: Exceptional teaching case covering cryptococcal meningitis in AIDS, ICP management as the key to survival, IRIS prevention by delaying ART, the danger of corticosteroids in crypto (unlike TBM), and the clinical pitfalls of absent meningismus and clear CSF in immunocompromised patients.

strengths: Masterfully constructed case that teaches multiple critical clinical pitfalls
Red herrings are realistic and clinically dangerous (absent meningismus, clear CSF)
Social history clues requiring HIV recognition are well-crafted
Follow-up outputs (therapeutic LP, fungal culture, ophthalmology, chest CT) are excellent
The contrast between ICP management in crypto vs TBM is a high-yield teaching point
IRIS prevention by delaying ART is a life-saving clinical pearl
